title: Incursa.Quic Public API Surface
purpose: Define the intended public API surface for Incursa.Quic using the public surface of System.Net.Quic as the baseline for the initial consumer cut.
scope: This specification defines the initial consumer-facing public API surface for Incursa.Quic. The repository is implementing the surface in phases, and the first shipped slice is the connection-side facade over the existing runtime and stream machinery. The slice includes the approved facade types, but it defers listener/socket wiring, the client and server TLS-auth option wrappers, and the connect/listen/accept entry points to the next slice. Helper-layer packet, frame, TLS, handshake, runtime, recovery, and congestion types remain internal unless a future specification explicitly promotes them.
context: The reference surface in Microsoft System.Net.Quic is the strongest baseline for the eventual API, but this repository is deliberately shipping that surface in smaller phases. The target repository already contains a broad internal helper and runtime surface under src/Incursa.Quic, and this specification makes the public/internal boundary explicit so the connection-first facade can land without exposing the implementation seams.

requirements.title: Limit the public consumer surface to the approved facade types
requirements.statement: The library MUST expose only the consumer-facing facade types named in this specification, and it MUST keep all other current helper, parser, runtime, TLS, handshake, packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and seam types internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes them.
notes: The approved public facade for the current slice is QuicConnection, QuicStream, QuicConnectionOptions, QuicReceiveWindowSizes, QuicAbortDirection, QuicError, QuicException, and the corrected QuicStreamType. Listener, client/server TLS wrappers, the callback args type, and the connect/listen/accept entry points remain deferred to the next slice.

The current slice reuses the existing runtime and stream-state machinery directly. The next slice can introduce EndPoint, IPEndPoint, SslApplicationProtocol, SslClientAuthenticationOptions, SslServerAuthenticationOptions, and SslClientHelloInfo plumbing when listener and connect work lands.

requirements.title: Split client and server entry points
requirements.statement: The library MUST expose client connection establishment through QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(QuicClientConnectionOptions, CancellationToken) and server listener creation and accept through QuicListener.ListenAsync(QuicListenerOptions, CancellationToken) and QuicListener.AcceptConnectionAsync(CancellationToken), with client-specific and server-specific settings kept in distinct options types.
notes: Listener setup, accept flow, and the server-side callback are intentionally deferred from this slice.
This slice only establishes the connection-side facade and the public boundary trim; the next slice can add the listener shell and its server-option callback.

requirements.title: Keep the connection model opaque and consumer-facing
requirements.statement: The QuicConnection type MUST represent a connected QUIC session and expose the local endpoint, remote endpoint, target host name, negotiated application protocol, negotiated cipher suite, negotiated TLS protocol, remote certificate, AcceptInboundStreamAsync, OpenOutboundStreamAsync, CloseAsync, DisposeAsync, and ToString, while keeping handshake details, packet state, runtime state, and transport-helper state out of the public surface.
notes: The connection object is the consumer's lifecycle anchor for this slice and may be created only by internal wiring for now.
Transport internals should be accessed through the internal runtime, not through public connection properties.
Connection close, dispose, and terminal-state projection are the only lifecycle behaviors this slice promises.
The connection object is not a middleware or event-pipeline surface.

requirements.title: Keep the stream model aligned with standard .NET stream consumers
requirements.statement: The QuicStream type MUST derive from Stream, expose the stream identifier, stream direction, closed-completion tasks, abort and graceful write-completion operations, and the standard synchronous, asynchronous, span-based, capability, and timeout overrides used by Stream.
notes: The public QuicStreamType enum MUST use the direction-only model with Bidirectional and Unidirectional values.
The richer internal stream-identity model may continue to track initiator detail separately, but it stays internal.

This slice only promises the read-side and terminal-state surface that is backed by QuicConnectionStreamState; write-heavy and abort-heavy stream behavior stays deferred until the stream-action pipeline exists.
The Stream surface stays internal to the facade for now, and the next slice can decide which timeout and write members are honest once stream action exists.

requirements.title: Expose configuration through the approved option and callback types
requirements.statement: The library MUST carry configuration through QuicConnectionOptions, QuicClientConnectionOptions, QuicServerConnectionOptions, QuicListenerOptions, and QuicReceiveWindowSizes, and it MUST expose only the approved knobs on those types: close and stream default error codes, handshake and idle timeouts, keep-alive interval, inbound stream limits, stream-capacity callback, client authentication options, server authentication options, listener endpoint and backlog, application protocols, and the listener connection-options callback.
notes: No custom endpoint or handshake wrapper types are introduced for this slice; the public surface only reuses the internal runtime and stream-state seams.
Public option objects are configuration bags, not live mutable protocol state.
Listener setup, client connect, and the listener connection-options callback are deferred to the next slice.
StreamCapacityCallback is also deferred until the public stream-capacity path exists end to end.

requirements.title: Keep the close and error surface explicit
requirements.statement: The library MUST classify terminal and shutdown outcomes through QuicError and QuicException, surface application and transport error codes on the exception, and support per-stream abort direction through QuicAbortDirection so consumers can distinguish read, write, and both-side termination without referencing internal protocol state.
notes: Connection shutdown remains available through QuicConnection.CloseAsync(long errorCode, CancellationToken) and DisposeAsync.
The exception surface is the consumer-facing representation of terminal outcomes, not a protocol debug channel.
CloseAsync validates the application error code and throws ArgumentOutOfRangeException for invalid values.
CloseAsync is terminal and repeated close calls are ignored once the connection is already closed.
DisposeAsync is the consumer-facing cleanup path when no explicit close is issued; it uses the configured default close error code.
Stream abort behavior is deferred until the write-side stream-action pipeline exists.

requirements.title: Keep the TLS, handshake, packet, and runtime seams internal
requirements.statement: The library MUST keep QuicTlsTranscriptProgress, QuicTlsKeySchedule, QuicTransportTlsBridgeState, QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator, QuicInitialPacketProtection, QuicHandshakePacketProtection, QuicTlsPacketProtectionMaterial, QuicConnectionRuntime, QuicConnectionRuntimeHost, QuicConnectionRuntimeShard, and QuicConnectionSendRuntime internal, and it MUST keep the remainder of the packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and stream-identity helpers internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes them.
notes: This boundary keeps transcript progress, key schedule, bridge state, handshake coordination, packet-protection details, runtime reducer details, stream-state helpers, packet helpers, and transport-parameter helpers out of the consumer contract.
The public API must not expose the existing helper-layer runtime model by accident.

requirements.title: Honor async lifecycle cancellation and terminal-state transitions
requirements.statement: The library MUST honor cancellation tokens on pending connect, listen, accept, open-stream, and close operations, and the server connection-options callback MUST observe the cancellation token supplied by the listener accept flow. A pre-canceled or later-canceled token MUST complete the still-pending operation with OperationCanceledException, but once a connection or stream has committed or reached a terminal state the public API MUST complete with the documented QuicError or ObjectDisposedException outcome instead of silently reviving or hiding the operation.
notes: The Microsoft tests directly prove cancellation for listener accept, the listener callback, and blocked outbound stream opens; the client connect path uses the same async contract shape and should remain cancellation-aware.
Cancellation does not retroactively undo a committed connection, accepted stream, or completed close.
Idempotent close and dispose semantics are consumer-facing terminal behavior, not a hidden runtime optimization.

supplemental_sections.heading: Public Surface Inventory
supplemental_sections.content:
- `QuicListener`: server-side accept/listen entry point.
- `QuicConnection`: connected session facade.
- `QuicStream`: standard `Stream` facade for a QUIC stream.
- `QuicConnectionOptions`: shared connection knobs.
- `QuicClientConnectionOptions`: client-only connect options.
- `QuicServerConnectionOptions`: server-only accept/listen options.
- `QuicListenerOptions`: listener configuration and server connection-options callback.
- `QuicReceiveWindowSizes`: connection and stream receive-window settings.
- `QuicAbortDirection`: stream abort direction.
- `QuicError` and `QuicException`: close and terminal error surface.
- `QuicStreamType`: bidirectional/unidirectional stream direction.
- `QuicStreamCapacityChangedArgs`: callback payload for stream-capacity notifications.

The public API should reuse `EndPoint`, `IPEndPoint`, `SslApplicationProtocol`, `SslClientAuthenticationOptions`, `SslServerAuthenticationOptions`, and `SslClientHelloInfo` directly rather than introduce new endpoint or TLS wrapper abstractions.

supplemental_sections.heading: Behavioral Evidence From Microsoft Tests
supplemental_sections.content:
The following Microsoft library-focused tests were the behavioral baseline for this pass:

- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicListenerTests.cs`: listener setup validation, callback exception and timeout behavior, cancellation, backlog, ALPN, and listener-disposal behavior.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicConnectionTests.cs`: connect validation, close/dispose terminal-state behavior, stream-capacity callback deltas, peer-certificate lifetime, and peer-abort propagation.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicStreamTests.cs`: direction-based capabilities, EOF and graceful completion, abort mapping, completion-task behavior, cancellation, and stream-dispose behavior.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicStreamConnectedStreamConformanceTests.cs`: `QuicStream` participates in the standard timeout-aware `Stream` contract.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/MsQuicCipherSuitesPolicyTests.cs`: the API reuses BCL TLS options directly and surfaces invalid cipher-suite combinations through public exceptions.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/MsQuicTests.cs`: idle timeout, blocked outbound stream open cancellation, and idempotent close behavior.

These tests justified the added lifecycle and cancellation language in `REQ-QUIC-API-0008` and the tightened validation notes in `REQ-QUIC-API-0005` and `REQ-QUIC-API-0006`.

supplemental_sections.heading: Internal-Only Surface
supplemental_sections.content:
The current helper and runtime seam types stay internal, including `QuicTlsTranscriptProgress`, `QuicTlsKeySchedule`, `QuicTransportTlsBridgeState`, `QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator`, `QuicInitialPacketProtection`, `QuicHandshakePacketProtection`, `QuicTlsPacketProtectionMaterial`, `QuicConnectionRuntime`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeHost`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeShard`, `QuicConnectionSendRuntime`, and `QuicConnectionRuntimeDeadlineScheduler`.

The remainder of the packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and stream-identity helpers also remain internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes one of them.

supplemental_sections.heading: Deviation Policy
supplemental_sections.content:
No intentional deviations from the Microsoft ref surface are required for this initial cut.

The repo-specific rule is that the richer internal transport engine stays hidden behind the consumer facade, and the public surface does not grow into a general middleware model.
